pattern: Scanning the queries backwards turns "later queries overwrite earlier ones" into "the first time from the end that a row or column is touched is the only time that matters", so each row and column contributes exactly once, weighted by however many opposite-axis cells are still unclaimed at that moment.
hints: A query only matters for the cells it is the LAST one to touch -- so think about processing the queries from the end of the list backwards instead of from the start.
Keep track of which rows and which columns have already been claimed (finalized) as you scan backwards; a query on an already-claimed row or column can be skipped entirely.
When you claim a fresh row with value v, it contributes v times however many columns are still unclaimed at that moment (and symmetrically for a fresh column) -- sum these contributions as you scan.

edge_inputs.description: small mix of row and column queries
row queries and column queries interleaved differently
2x2 matrix with only a column ever touched
same row queried twice, only the later value should count
row then column then the same row again -- naive forward summation double counts the first row write
value of zero written last on a row must still finalize it (later zero beats an earlier nonzero)
larger matrix with every row and every column eventually touched

stress.description: random small matrices with a healthy mix of row/column queries, kept small so the O(n^2) simulation oracle stays fast
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = min(max(size, 1), 30)
    q_count = min(200, max(1, n * 5))
    queries = []
    for _ in range(q_count):
        t = rng.randint(0, 1)
        idx = rng.randint(0, n - 1)
        val = rng.randint(0, 100000)
        queries.append([t, idx, val])
    return [n, queries]
